optionFeedback.feedback: Correct. Swing plus improvised solos over a groove is the signature of jazz.
Incorrect. Baroque uses pre-written interwoven lines, not live improvised solos.
Incorrect. Identifying genres by ear is exactly the skill being built.
Incorrect. A Romantic symphony is large, fully notated, and not built on swung improvisation.

successCriteria: Distinguishes jazz by ear via swing and improvised soloing.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option and (if prompted) can justify why each distractor is wrong.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option without full justification.
rubric.weak: Selects a distractor, revealing the linked misconception.

errorPatterns.description: Learner cannot place a genre by ear or treats boundaries as unknowable.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: trace one lineage (gospel to soul) and identify an audible borrowed technique

questions.prompt: Kendrick Lamar's 2018 Pulitzer Prize for Music is significant mainly because it shows what?
options: That classical music is serious and rap is simple.
That craft and importance exist across all genres, dissolving the 'serious vs simple' hierarchy.
That he was simply the most popular artist that year.
That hip-hop has no connection to earlier music.

optionFeedback.feedback: Incorrect. The award proves the reverse of this prejudice.
Correct. It was the first such Pulitzer to a non-classical/jazz artist, validating cross-genre craft.
Incorrect. The prize honors artistic significance, not chart popularity.
Incorrect. His work fuses jazz, funk, and soul - deeply connected to earlier music.

successCriteria: Rejects the 'classical serious / popular simple' hierarchy.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option and (if prompted) can justify why each distractor is wrong.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option without full justification.
rubric.weak: Selects a distractor, revealing the linked misconception.

errorPatterns.description: Learner ranks classical above popular music as inherently more serious.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: analyze rhyme/rhythm complexity in a hip-hop verse beside a Baroque texture; cite the Pulitzer

questions.prompt: Pick two genres from the course (e.g. soul and rock). In three to four sentences, give one by-ear cue for telling each apart, and explain why the boundary between them is a convention rather than a hard wall.
successCriteria: Gives a valid by-ear cue per genre
Explains genre boundaries as porous conventions

rubric.excellent: Provides a correct distinguishing cue for each genre AND explains the boundary as porous, ideally citing a shared ancestor.
rubric.adequate: Cues are mostly correct but the 'conventions not walls' point is underdeveloped.
rubric.weak: Treats genres as sealed boxes or gives incorrect cues.

errorPatterns.description: Answer treats genres as rigidly separate with no shared lineage.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: trace a concrete cross-genre lineage and name a borrowed technique

questions.prompt: Which best reflects how to judge Louis Armstrong's most IMPORTANT work?
options: His most important work is 'What a Wonderful World' because it's his most famous.
His revolutionary importance lies in his 1920s Hot Five and Hot Seven recordings, regardless of later chart fame.
Importance and popularity are always the same thing.
He has no signature works worth naming.

optionFeedback.feedback: Incorrect. Fame and historical significance differ; his late hit is not his most pivotal work.
Correct. The 1920s recordings reinvented jazz and define his significance.
Incorrect. Popularity and significance are separate axes that frequently diverge.
Incorrect. He has clearly identifiable signature recordings.

successCriteria: Separates an artist's most significant work from their most popular one.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option and (if prompted) can justify why each distractor is wrong.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option without full justification.
rubric.weak: Selects a distractor, revealing the linked misconception.

errorPatterns.description: Learner picks the most famous work as the most important.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: show the popularity-vs-significance split and ask for the pivotal work

questions.prompt: Name one signature work for any TWO course artists, and for each state one specific thing a listener should listen for in it.
successCriteria: Names a valid signature work per artist
States a specific, accurate listening focus per work

rubric.excellent: Two correct work-artist pairings, each with an accurate and specific listening cue.
rubric.adequate: Two pairings correct but at least one listening cue is vague.
rubric.weak: Names only most-famous-but-not-signature works, or listening cues are missing/incorrect.

errorPatterns.description: Answer defaults to best-known rather than signature works.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: resurface significance-vs-popularity contrast for the named artist

questions.prompt: In the course's 'rhythm relay', which ordering correctly traces rhythm moving toward the center of the music?
options: Bach's motor -> Stravinsky's rhythmic rupture -> jazz swing -> hip-hop's looped beat.
Hip-hop -> jazz -> Stravinsky -> Bach.
Dylan's lyrics -> Aretha's voice -> Bach's harmony.
The elements are unrelated and can't be sequenced.

optionFeedback.feedback: Correct. This is the chronological handoff of rhythm from background to foundation.
Incorrect. This reverses the actual chronological lineage.
Incorrect. Those belong to the individual-voice thread and are out of order.
Incorrect. Tracing influence across eras is exactly this skill.

successCriteria: Sequences a cross-era influence chain correctly.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option and (if prompted) can justify why each distractor is wrong.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option without full justification.
rubric.weak: Selects a distractor, revealing the linked misconception.

errorPatterns.description: Learner mis-sequences or reverses the era chain.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: have learner state the distinct problem each era solved

questions.prompt: In hip-hop, 'sampling' most directly demonstrates which course-long principle?
options: That genres appear from nowhere as sealed originals.
That musical lineage is real and, here, literally audible inside the new track.
That newer music is always more advanced than older music.
That rhythm doesn't matter in modern music.

optionFeedback.feedback: Incorrect. Sampling shows the opposite - audible lineage from older records.
Correct. A looped soul or jazz fragment embeds the genealogy directly in the music.
Incorrect. That is the ladder myth the course rejects.
Incorrect. Rhythm is hip-hop's foundation, not absent.

successCriteria: Connects sampling to audible cross-era lineage.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option and (if prompted) can justify why each distractor is wrong.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option without full justification.
rubric.weak: Selects a distractor, revealing the linked misconception.

errorPatterns.description: Learner denies audible lineage between genres.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: play a hip-hop track over an audible soul/jazz sample and name the source

questions.prompt: Explain in four to five sentences why 'Western music got steadily more advanced in a straight line' is the wrong model for this course. Use at least two artists who solved DIFFERENT problems rather than one improving on the other.
successCriteria: Rejects linear-progress model
Names two artists solving different problems
Frames history as branching/cross-pollinating

rubric.excellent: Clearly rejects the ladder model, names two artists solving genuinely different problems, and frames the history as a branching tree.
rubric.adequate: Rejects the ladder but the two-artist contrast is thin or only one problem is articulated.
rubric.weak: Endorses or fails to challenge the straight-line model.

errorPatterns.description: Answer endorses linear progress / each era beating the last.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: resurface branching-tree framing and the different-problems exercise

questions.prompt: A friend says 'I tried that famous piece once, didn't get it, so it's clearly not for me.' Best response?
options: They're right - if you don't get it immediately, it isn't for you.
Rewarding listening is built through preparation and repeated, attentive passes - like learning to taste wine.
They should read sheet music first or give up.
Only naturally gifted people can enjoy that piece.

optionFeedback.feedback: Incorrect. This is the 'first listen or never' misconception.
Correct. Prepared, repeated attention is how rich listening is built.
Incorrect. Notation is not a prerequisite for rich listening.
Incorrect. Prepared listening is a learnable skill, not an inborn gift.

successCriteria: Frames rich listening as prepared and repeated, not first-pass.

rubric.excellent: Selects the correct option and (if prompted) can justify why each distractor is wrong.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct option without full justification.
rubric.weak: Selects a distractor, revealing the linked misconception.

errorPatterns.description: Learner abandons a piece after one unprepared listen.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: assign a written listening brief plus a prepared re-listen

questions.prompt: Write a short 'listening brief' (five to six sentences) for one course artist you could hear live or stream tonight: (1) era and context, (2) what made them innovative versus contemporaries, (3) one signature work and one thing to listen for, (4) one earlier course influence you expect to hear, (5) two listening elements you'll deliberately spotlight.
successCriteria: Covers context, innovation, a signature work + cue, an earlier influence, and two targeted elements

rubric.excellent: All five components present, accurate, specific, and integrated into a usable concert brief that draws on multiple competencies.
rubric.adequate: Most components present but one or two are thin, generic, or slightly inaccurate.
rubric.weak: Missing several components or treats the piece as needing no preparation.

errorPatterns.description: Brief shows no preparation strategy / assumes instant comprehension.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: model a completed brief and require a prepared re-listen

errorPatterns.description: Brief omits context entirely.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: resurface context-card pairing for the chosen artist

glossary.definition: Directing attention to specific musical events as they occur; a learnable skill distinct from passive enjoyment.
A sequence of single pitches heard as a connected tune - the part you hum.
The pattern of sounds and silences in time; the part that makes you tap your foot.
The speed of the underlying beat - how fast or slow the music goes.
Two or more pitches sounding at once, and how those combinations move and change.
Pitch combinations that sound settled or sweet.
Pitch combinations that sound tense or clashing - used deliberately for effect, not a mistake.
Tone color; the quality that distinguishes two sound sources playing the same pitch (e.g. trumpet vs flute).
Levels of loudness and softness, and the changes between them.
The number of simultaneous musical layers and how they relate (thin vs thick).
The overall organization or shape of a piece over time (e.g. verse-chorus).
Era of Western music c. 1600-1750, marked by intricate decoration and dense, interwoven complexity.
The art of combining two or more independent melodies that work together while each stays interesting alone.
A contrapuntal form where one melodic subject is introduced alone, then layered against itself in overlapping entries.
Era c. 1750-1820 valuing balance, clarity, and elegant form.
Era following the Classical, valuing intense personal emotion and individual expression.
A texture with one clear lead tune supported by backing - typical of Classical/Romantic music.
Early-20th-century movement that broke deliberately with Romantic conventions, emphasizing rhythm, dissonance, and new sounds.
Stravinsky's 1913 ballet whose premiere caused a near-riot; a landmark of rhythmic and dissonant innovation.
The deliberate use of timbre - unusual instrument ranges and combinations - as a central creative element.
Black American music built from work songs, hollers, and church singing; defined by blue notes, call-and-response, and the AAB verse; the root feeding jazz, soul, and rock.
A pitch deliberately bent or flattened, living between the notes of the European scale; central to blues expression and impossible to capture fully in notation.
A musical conversation in which a sung or played phrase is answered by another voice or instrument; inherited from church and work-song traditions.
The classic blues lyric shape: a line stated, repeated, then answered by a payoff line.
The 1920s industry category for records by Black artists marketed to Black audiences; the commercial doorway through which blues and early jazz reached mass listeners.
Music born in early-20th-century African American New Orleans, defined by improvisation and swing.
Composing and performing music spontaneously, in real time.
A loose, springy, propulsive way of bending rhythm that makes jazz lilt and move.
A passage where one player steps forward to improvise while the others support.
Improvised, wordless vocal singing using nonsense syllables, using the voice like an instrument.
Fast, complex, virtuosic 1940s jazz.
Relaxed, spacious, understated jazz that reacted against bebop's heat.
Jazz built on slow-moving scales rather than fast chord changes, opening space for calm improvisation; landmark album Kind of Blue.
Jazz blended with electric instruments, rock energy, and funk grooves.
Building a recording in layers to create sounds impossible in a single live performance.
A multi-track record designed to be heard as a unified artistic statement.
Guitar/bass/drums band music with a strong backbeat and increasingly elaborate studio production.
Gospel-rooted, voice-forward popular music full of emotional runs and groove.
Black American church music featuring call-and-response and improvised vocal runs; the root of soul.
Stretching a single syllable across many pitches - a hallmark of gospel and soul singing.
Acoustic, plain, often topical 'music of the people,' with lyrics carrying the weight.
Song words written with the depth and ambition of poetry.
Music from the 1970s Bronx built primarily on rhythm and words, with melody/harmony often a supporting loop.
The looped instrumental foundation of a hip-hop track.
Taking a snippet of an existing recording and repurposing it as material for a new track - making lineage literally audible.
A rapper's rhythmic, rhymed vocal delivery and how it rides the beat; hip-hop's signature rhythmic feel.
An album unified by a single narrative or theme - an inheritance from the Beatles' album-as-art.
The model that music evolves by branching and cross-pollinating, not by linear 'progress' where each era beats the last.
